audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed as Elihu's first direct address to Job: appeal to shared clay, claim that God speaks through dreams and suffering, messenger/mediator imagery, ransom language, restoration, and Elihu's invitation for reply.

verses.restored: But now, Job, please hear my words, and listen to all my speech.
Look, I have opened my mouth; my tongue has spoken in my palate.
My words are from the uprightness of my heart, and my lips speak knowledge clearly.
The Spirit of the Divine has made me, and the breath of Shaddai gives me life.
If you are able, answer me; arrange yourself before me, take your stand.
Look, I am like you before the Divine; I too was pinched off from clay.
Look, my terror will not frighten you, and my pressure will not be heavy upon you.
Surely you have spoken in my ears, and I have heard the sound of words:
I am pure, without transgression; I am clean, and there is no guilt in me.
Look, he finds occasions against me; he counts me as his enemy.
He puts my feet in the stocks; he watches all my paths.
Look, in this you are not right, I answer you, for the Divine is greater than a mortal.
Why do you contend against him, saying that he answers none of a person’s words?
For the Divine speaks in one way, and in two, though a person does not perceive it.
In a dream, in a vision of the night, when deep sleep falls on people, while they slumber on the bed,
then he opens the ear of people and seals their instruction,
to turn a person from deed, and hide pride from a man.
He keeps back his soul from the pit, and his life from crossing the weapon.
He is also corrected with pain on his bed, and with continual strife in his bones,
so that his life loathes bread, and his soul desirable food.
His flesh wastes away from sight, and his bones once unseen are exposed.
His soul draws near to the pit, and his life to those who bring death.
If there is for him a messenger, a mediator, one out of a thousand, to declare to a human what is right for him,
then he is gracious to him and says, Ransom him from going down to the pit; I have found a ransom.
His flesh becomes fresher than a child’s; he returns to the days of his youth.
He prays to the Divine, and the Divine accepts him; he sees his face with a shout of joy, and he restores to a person his righteousness.
He sings before people and says, I sinned and twisted what was right, but it was not repaid to me.
He redeemed my soul from passing into the pit, and my life shall see the light.
Look, all these the Divine does, two times, three times with a person,
to bring back his soul from the pit, to be illumined with the light of life.
Pay attention, Job; listen to me. Be silent, and I myself will speak.
If you have words, answer me; speak, for I desire to justify you.
If not, listen to me; be silent, and I will teach you wisdom.
